title: 15 Things Everyone Believes That Data Contradicts

findings.myth: The War on Drugs reduces drug deaths
findings.reality: Overdose deaths rose 5.6x while enforcement spending rose 4x. YoY correlation ≈ 0.

findings.myth: Tough-on-crime drug policy reduces violent crime
findings.reality: Drug war spending has near-zero YoY correlation with violent crime rates.
No meaningful YoY correlation between enforcement spending and median income.
Tariff increases correlate with higher consumer prices, not higher income.
Near-zero YoY correlation between minimum wage changes and unemployment.

findings.myth: Lower tax rates increase tax revenue
findings.reality: Federal revenue stays at 15-20% GDP regardless of top rate (91% or 37%).

findings.myth: More healthcare spending means better outcomes
findings.reality: US spends 2.2x OECD average, gets 2.4 fewer years life expectancy.
Crime drives spending (reactive), not the reverse. YoY correlation ≈ 0.
US has 5x incarceration of peers with similar crime. YoY r = 0.09-0.23.

findings.myth: Gun ownership directly predicts homicide rates
findings.reality: Only 23 data points; near-zero YoY correlation. Confounded by poverty and urbanization.
Absolute r = -0.655 vanishes to r = -0.013 in YoY. Entirely spurious.
Aid correlates positively with conflict (r = +0.715) — it's reactive, not preventive.
Strongest causal finding: abstinence funding INCREASES teen pregnancy (r = +0.713).
Complete null — no correlation between regulatory volume and GDP/business formation.
Zero GDP harm from renewables. Strong CO2 reduction (r = -0.960, trend-driven).

summary.topPattern: Confusing correlation with causation from monotonic trends
summary.secondPattern: Reversed causation — spending is reactive, not causal
